item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have a little question.

Interview Log / POI#3694

Isn't it Interview Log / POI#3674?
Or intended?
I think it's mistype.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Upvoting, but I've got some big complaints. One I feel is just my personal opinions, and nothing to be done about that; but the other, I feel is without explanation or just out of foundation character.

The first bit: I feel like gamers against weed is going down the are we cool yet and wondertainment road of overuse as a goi. It's getting to the point where it just feels like its tacked on, like, why can't there be other online communities of individuals like this, why is it always them.

But that's just me, and I know plenty of people have no problem with it.

Now, the second problem: why is it just assumed that closing or destroying the school would cause it to move elsewhere? Why is the researcher just assuming this? Is there precedence? Did someone tell them this? Because as it stands there is no reason indicated that it would be like this. I can't imagine the foundation wouldn't have tried to close the school on some fabricated basis to see whether removing public access to see if it would contain it from the public. It would make sense to me if they tried to do this and found it starting to appear elsewhere, leading them to re-open the school and expand their janitorial activities to both places, or leaving the school shut down and moving the janitor operation there. I know the purpose of this is to justify why they are patrolling hallways for these, and that's fine, but as it is it doesn't make sense in universe.

Overall though, I liked it. At least enough to +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Please don’t upvote your own article.

This has been allowed for a while now. Many authors do it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: +1, but it could still use some work, as others have said. My biggest problem is that the ending edit makes a lot of assumptions without reason, but I guess that’s why it’s not in the article. I think it should include this in the final statement: instead of just “pending approval”, maybe a “however, due to” note.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Gonna echo sentiments here and just say that this has potential to be a great article. I would somehow save the criticism given by others here so you could make changes based on that
Afterwards, I'd run this through another round of critique on the forum or chat.

Just don't give up and you could make something amazing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I assumed that it was more realistic based around an email-like messaging system.

I don’t really expect the reader to read the whole block of that O5-1 through O5-13, but I assumed the reader would look at it, register that the message was to O5 Command, and continue with reading the message.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I mostly like it, but one thing I noticed was this:

To: O5-1, O5-2, O5-3, O5-4, O5-5, O5-6, O5-7, O5-8, O5-9, O5-10, O5-11, O5-12, O5-13

Why not just

To: O5 Command

or something similar?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I’ve had a couple people tell me that I should rewrite the testing logs to make the white guys hallucinations instead of solid figures. From your feedback, I think that may be what I’ll do.

I think a big problem with the concept— the fact that the white guys themselves are creating and hanging up the posters— is the worst part of the article. That was supposed to be the big twist: the SCPs got out of control, and if they get outside the school, there would be no telling what would happen, so the Foundation needs to keep them inside.
I wish I knew a good way to go about doing that twist, but that ending is the best I could come up with so far.

I’m sure I’ll figure out something to do with it. Thanks for your feedback.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A couple people suggested that the number (#) be taken out before you, and I kept it in just because I felt it added something to the document.

But I think enough people pointed it out that I’m gonna get rid of it, whether in the draft form or on the site if it doesn’t get deleted.

Otherwise, I’ll attempt to defend the time approximations one last time, although others have pointed that out as well (and I’m sure I’ll change it to be less specific): every time was recorded during a testing log, so I think that the specific times should be as specific as possible. If each time was recorded in the testing, then I would put the approximate time in the description.
However, I’ve received enough feedback to know that it clogs the article, and I’ll probably change it around.

Anyway, thanks for your feedback!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm afraid this is gonna have to be a -1 from me.

+ show block
– hide block

There are quite a few grammar and clinical tone errors scattered throughout the article:

instances do not have to materialize at every single time marker;

After approximately thirty-two minutes and twenty-four seconds

Attempts to translate the binary into anything meaningful always results in gibberish; this hints that the code is, indeed, meaningless. Testing is still ongoing, but is considered low-priority.

Are a few examples. For reference, I would replace each one respectively with:

instances do not necessarily materialize each day;

This sounds a bit better in my opinion, as 'do not have to' makes it seem like some sort of rule, and 'time markers' is an odd way to phrase it.

After approximately thirty minutes

The original sounded off to me because of how specific it was - despite you saying 'approximately'. This works better, or you could just get rid of the approximately.

Binary translates into seemingly random strings of numbers and letters.

Don't use 'gibberish' in a clinical document, and the hinting at meaningless binary bit is pointless. Its very obviously meaningless from the start.

Like I said, these are just a few examples - there are certainly more bits needing improvement. But another issue is that it's simply too long.

More text doesn't necessarily make a better article. As is, its quite difficult to get through, and would be vastly improved by cutting out some of the filler, particularly in the test logs. There are also some conceptual issues, but to be honest I've never been great at concepts in general.

I would recommend getting it fixed up in the draft forum. Also, make sure you look over some of the guides in the guide hub.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I upvoted, but there's some questionable logic. First, the Foundation sent in an entire MTF without an extraction plan. Second, the idea that it will spread to another area lacks any evidence.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
One (1) janitorial uniform consistent with the uniforms given to custodians of the school, consisting of: one (1) pair of navy blue pants, one (1) navy blue jacket with the lettering: “███████ High School” on the back, one (1) brown or black belt, and one (1) white or gray dress shirt.

This "number (#)" notation ruins articles for me. I understand that apparently, it's some sort of actual thing that's done in legal documents or whatever to make sure that number amounts are made perfectly clear. Something like that. But it just always comes across as unnecessary junk text that disrupts the flow of the document, especially when multiple numbers come up in a small amount of space. It makes me want to stop reading, almost.

Other than my annoyance with that notation, I think the document itself is decently presented. The tone isn't bad and I didn't notice any particularly egregious issues. The anomaly itself doesn't really do much for me, and there's some issues I have with a few things, like the very specific "approximations" of the time that it takes for things to happen, the fact that the Foundation felt the need to have three armed agents present in that first test, and the lengthy explanation of details of the anomaly that probably would've been better left unexplained, among other things.

It's a big article, I don't want to go line-by-line picking apart everything I didn't like about it. But the long and short of it is that I couldn't like this one. -1.

item.title: SCP-3680
item.content:encoded:
My first skip. If anything, I hope it floats.

If you have any changes you think would make it better, let me know. If this post does end up like the Titanic, I might as well make it a learning experience, maybe salvage the rubble so to speak.

Thanks for taking the time to read it (because I know it's a bit lengthy)!
